speaker: interviewer
text: We're in the lovely home of Nuremberg court reporter, Vivien Spitz, in Aurora, Colorado, United States of America. My name is Gary Lubell, and we're conducting this interview in English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Vivien, could you share with me a little bit more about the international nature of this trial and-- and what that was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the major trial-- Goering's trial, the major Nazi leaders-- was the International Military Tribunal trial. And as I said, when that trial ended, then the 12 subsequent trials were prosecuted by only the United States. In the case of each of the 12 subsequent trials, the title was the United States of America versus. In the medical case, Karl Brandt et alia-- [? and there ?] meaning and others. And-- but I understand that they were still considered a military tribunal. That might not have been an international.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, prior to the time you arrived in-- in the-- the preceding trial, what-- what is it that you became aware of, as you entered into the doctor's trials, about what had proceeded in that courtroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had-- we still had some of the court reporters who worked on the major trial. And as we developed this little group of friends, then those of us who were new for the Doctors' Trial learned from them. When we met in the evenings, usually in the Grand Hotel, you know, we-- they-- they would talk about what had transpired, and we learned that way. We also learned in our office, reporting office, just through discussions about what had happened. And we were developing, certainly, an awareness about how horrible this testimony was going to be. That we really didn't know. And there are only two groups of people, I believe, who did not know what we were going to hear in Nuremberg, and they were the court reporters and the judges before the actual-- really knew what we were going to hear. Because the-- the prosecutors, the defense counsel, all the lawyers, the interpreters, the interrogators, the translators, the document examiners, they all knew what the evidence was going to be that came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that had not been shared with-- with the court reporters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- no, not really. Because we had not worked on anything up to that point, where they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you involved, prior to the actual trial beginning, in taking any depositions, or involved in any interviews of witnesses, or anything like that, where you-- you reported, so you-- you had any clue what was-- what these trials would be about?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not take any of the depositions. Two of the reporters in my Detroit office-- the older reporters, husband and wife team-- went to-- to Dachau before I ever went over there and before the major trial started. And they reported these pretrial hearings in Dachau. But they did not come to Nuremberg to work on those trials. We did not interchange reporters there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, when the actual Doctors' Trials began, what are your recollections of-- of what took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Doctors' Trial began, and whenever, in the beginning, I went into that courtroom, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just especially the testimony. Taking the testimony. And I remember sitting there-- and across from me were the doctors, and the German defense counsel in front of them-- studying the faces of those doctors. And I just had the feeling that I never saw such evil faces in my life. And eyes. When you look at someone's eyes, there was a feeling of their resentment at even being there. It was just pervasive in that courtroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the actual charge? What they were charged with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With crimes against humanity, calculated genocide, with the performing experiments on the concentration camp inmates without their consent in the name of scientific medical [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] research.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there specific experiments that were to be tried?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were. They-- these-- pardon me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were 20 doctors and three medical assistants who were tried. All men and one woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have them. You know, when we get to the-- the slides and the pictures. I cannot name them all off right now, but Karl Brandt was the-- the chief of the medical services. He was the number one defendant, Hitler's personal physician. He was the doctor who set up all of the programs and all of the machinery [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for the medical experiments to be conducted at Auschwitz, Buchenwald, Dachau, Ravensbruck, Natzweiler and Sachsenhausen and other places. But he was the top man. And there were-- these doctors, many of them, were distinguished German scientists, doctors. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dr. Rose, in particular, I remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In universities, clinics, hospitals, all throughout Germany, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] charged with-- with these crimes. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they were charged with 12 major experiments. There were actually 14, but the final two were incorporated, really, into the other major-- these 12 major that I-- that I reported and talk about. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] With the outright murder of political prisoners, non-German nationals, intellectual dissidents, intellectual German dissidents, Catholic and Protestant clergy, and prisoners of war, especially Russian prisoners of war, through medical experimentation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Those who did not die or who were not deliberately murdered just suffered torture and maiming and permanent disfigurement and disability. They were ordered in wholesale lots. I remember the orders being-- especially had I become so aware of the anti-Semitism. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 200 Jews in good physical condition would be the order. 50 gypsies, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 500 tubercular Poles, 1,000 Russian prisoners of war. And the first experiment that they were charged with was high altitude, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was conducted at Dachau [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] in-- in the summer of 1942. And I thought at that time, that was the summer I graduated from high school. And this experiment was conducted to determine or study the limits of human existence and endurance at extremely high altitudes, up to 68,000 feet, without oxygen. And the victims were placed in airtight, ball-shaped compartments, and then high altitude atmospheric conditions were simulated by the alteration of pressure. Up to 68,000 feet, without oxygen. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was-- there was one exhibit that we had that was admitted into evidence, and I have a copy of it. It was Dr. Sigmund Rascher, who was so instrumental in-- in these experiments, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who was not tried because he was ultimately executed with his wife by-- at Hitler's order, for other reasons. But he submitted a report that I-- I refer to in my presentations. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Quote, 'It was a continuous experiment without oxygen, at a height of 12 kilometers, which is 39,000 feet, conducted on a 37-year-old Jew in good general condition. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Breathing continued up to 30 minutes. After four minutes, the experimental subject began to perspire and wiggle his head. After five minutes, cramps occurred. Between six and ten minutes, breathing increased in speed, and the experimental subject became unconscious. From 11 to 30 minutes, breathing slowed down to three breaths per minute, finally stopping altogether. Severe cyanosis developed in between, and foam appeared at the mouth.' Unquote. This was just one of the exhibits that was admitted in evidence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you are you aware of where that exhibit came from, and how that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a captured German document. The-- the Nazis had, fortunately, for the purpose of these trials, meticulously recorded by documentation, film, and photography, almost all of their atrocities, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whether they were in the medical case or the other cases. And because tens of thousands of pieces of documentary evidence had been captured by the British and US and Allied forces, most of the facts presented at these trials were incapable of being denied or defended. And in the Doctors' Trial, there were 85 witnesses or victims who testified, and there were 1,471 pieces of documentary evidence admitted into evidence. In that trial, there were over 11,000 pages of court transcript. And I learned that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for example, the entire foreign-- German foreign office documents were found by the Allied forces almost intact. They-- there were tons of documents that-- that were even located in abandoned castle walls in Germany, where the Nazis had tried to hide this evidence. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you this. The-- the defendants, when they were given the opportunity to enter their pleas, what took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all represented by counsel. And if-- and they were given time to get their own counsel. And if they had none, the American judges were prepared to appoint German counsel for them. Many of the German counsel were still members of the Nazi party. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they all entered their pleas without being allowed to make any statements. Not guilty. They all entered pleas of not guilty. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in the courtroom at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't believe that I had started the-- I do not remember this specifically, being in the courtroom at the time of the entry of the pleas.

speaker: interviewer
text: You described the-- the first experiment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you talk some about the other experiments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But I wanted to say first, in the high altitude experiment, these were approximately 200 experimental subjects, who were Russian and Polish Jews who-- and they were put into this experiment because they were termed professional criminals. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that meant they were professional criminals because they had been condemned for racial shame consisting of intercourse and marriage between non-Aryans and Aryans, which was the term given to pure-blooded Germans. And of that group of 200, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there were 80-- 70 to 80 who died in the experiment, and the others were just-- it was just pure torture for them. The second experiment was freezing or hypothermia, which was the experiment to which, over the many years, doctors in other parts of the world tried to look to find some-- something good that came out of this experiment. Hypothermia. And I think, for the most part, failed to find the merit. And I always thought it would have been very unethical anyway to even search, because of the methods used. But this-- this experiment, freezing was conducted from 1942 to 1943, which was my first year in college. And I thought about that when the years came out in the testimony. To determine the most effective means of heating severely chilled or frozen people. The victims-- I remember the photographs. They were placed in long, narrow tanks of ice water, and I can still see them. For up to three hours, at which point, death occurred, or by keeping them outdoors naked from 9 to 14 hours in sub-freezing temperatures. And the testimony that they screamed in pain as parts of their bodies froze. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then the rewarming of those who did not die was most effective by very hot baths. And then Heinrich Himmler, who was the chief of the Gestapo, a major Nazi leader who committed suicide after he was captured by the British, ordered rewarming by naked women. And I guess this was, maybe, one of the first truly shocking bits of testimony that I certainly heard. And at one point, four gypsy prostitutes, themselves prisoners, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were produced from Ravensbruck concentration camp to Dachau. And the chilled-to-unconsciousness male-- male, naked victim was placed between the two naked women, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just indicating how obscene all of this was. And I have, in my file in the other room, which I have not pulled for this purpose, a copy of that exhibit that was also offered and admitted into evidence. Captured German document. Describing, in graphic detail, graphic-- this experiment [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] signed by Dr. Sigmund Rascher to his superior, Heinrich Himmler, reporting all of the heil Hitler salutations and everything that went into their-- their terminology and their letterheads, and the addresses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- there were about 380 experimental subjects in this one. And there were-- experiments. I'm sorry. 380 experiments conducted on about 290 people. And there were 80 to 90 of those who died from this freezing experiment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the third experiment charged was malaria, conducted at Dachau, to investigate immunization for the treatment of malaria. There were 1,200 inmate victims who were selected German criminals, taken out of their own prisons, Russian prisoners of war, Yugoslavs, and a large number of Polish Catholic priests who were infected with malaria through controlled infected mosquito bites or by the injection of malaria-infected fresh blood. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of the 1,200 inmates, there were 14-- I mean, 400 deaths. And the others, of course, suffered the torture, the pain, and permanent disability in many cases. The fourth experiment charge was mustard gas, which was conducted to investigate the most effective treatment of mustard gas wounds. And one drop of a liquid gas substance was applied to the lower arm of the experimental subject. And 10 hours later, burns appeared and-- and spread over the entire body, wherever a liquid drop touched the skin. Photos were taken by the Nazis daily, which was captured German film used at the trials for conviction. The fatalities started occurring after the fifth day. Greater parts of the lungs and other internal organs were eaten away. Some of the experimental subjects had to enter the gas chambers and smash small ampules of liquid gas that they were provided, and inhaled vapors with the same result, lungs destroyed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were 220 victims, and 50 died almost immediately. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The fifth experiment was sulfanilamide, conducted to determine the effectiveness of sulfa. These victims included many Polish Catholic priests, who were wounded, the wounds infected with streptococcus, gas gangrene, and tetanus. And then, to simulate battle wounds, wood shavings and ground glass were forced into the wounds, resulting, of course, in intense agony and pain and mutilation, permanent disability, and countless deaths. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The sixth experiment [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was one of the most inhumane, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was bone, muscle, and nerve regeneration and transplantation. In which whole sections of bone-- whole legs, arms, including scapulas, were removed from healthy victims and attempted to be transplanted to other healthy victims, who, of necessity, had to have the same parts removed for this experiment. These were predominantly young Polish women. This resulted in death, but for those who lived, permanent mutilation and disability.

speaker: interviewee
text: On December 20, 1946, a 37-year-old Polish woman testified, during one of my turns in the courtroom, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as follows. And I have a bit of her testimony, which I have. 'Question-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what happened to you after you were arrested? Answer-- I was taken to the Gestapo. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Question-- Witness, you have told a tribunal that in July 1942, some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 75 Polish girls, who were in the transport from Lublin, were called before the camp doctors in Ravensbruck. Now, were any of these girls selected for an operation?'

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this young woman testified in Polish. And of course, it had to be interpreted into German and English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 'On this day, we did not know why we were called before the camp doctors. And on the same day, 10 of 25 girls were taken to the hospital, but we did not know why. We were called at a time when executions usually took place, and I thought I was going to be executed because some girls had been shot down before. In the hospital, we were put to bed. And the ward in which we stayed was locked. Then a German nurse arrived and gave me an injection in my leg. After this injection, I vomited and I was weak. Then I lost consciousness. I regained consciousness in the morning. And then I noticed that my leg was in a cast, from my ankle up to the knee. And I felt very great pain in this leg and had a high temperature. I noticed also that my leg was swollen, from the toes up to the groin. I felt great pain, and I had the impression that something must have been cut out of my leg. Two weeks later, the bandage was removed, and that was the first time I saw my leg. The incision was so deep that I could see the bone. The pus was draining from my leg, the leg was swollen up, and I could not walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Question-- how long was it until your leg was healed? Answer-- the pus was flowing from my leg till June 1943, and at that time, my wound was healed, one year later.' And I have a slide capture German film of this lady's leg, and testifying from the witness stand. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then the seventh experiment-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think we'll end tape four right here.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
